title: The Shattered Toast at Whitby Hall
openingIncident: At 8:45 pm, Eleanor Whitmore, renowned culinary critic, was found dead in the cosy kitchen table. Three people had been present during the final minutes, and each gave a different account. Nothing outside the room is needed to identify the killer.

suspects.name: Gareth Pembroke
suspects.statement: At 8:35 pm, Clara Haversham helped me set the bottle down on the side table. We left the room together immediately afterwards. Victor Langford and Eleanor Whitmore were still inside.
suspects.alibiClaim: Clara Haversham and I were together outside the room from 8:35 pm until the discovery at 8:45 pm.

suspects.name: Clara Haversham
suspects.statement: At 8:35 pm, while Gareth Pembroke set the bottle down on the side table, I set the glass down on the side table. We then left together and remained together until the body was found at 8:45 pm.
suspects.alibiClaim: Gareth Pembroke and I were together outside the room from 8:35 pm until the discovery at 8:45 pm.

suspects.name: Victor Langford
suspects.statement: I left at 8:30 pm. I never touched the knife and did not go near the glass.
suspects.alibiClaim: I had already left the room by 8:30 pm and did not return.

clues.title: The Edge of Knife
clues.detail: A fresh line of blood runs along one edge of the knife, and its point has a new bend. Eleanor Whitmore has a narrow wound consistent with that edge.

clues.title: Ring beneath Bottle
clues.detail: The fresh circular ring beneath the bottle is crossed by a narrow blood droplet from the attack whose edge remains unbroken. Gareth Pembroke and Clara Haversham both state that they set the bottle down on the side table at 8:35 pm before leaving together.

clues.title: Fibres on Knife
clues.detail: Teal-and-cream tweed fibres are caught around the handle of the knife. Victor Langford's teal tweed jacket cuff has a newly frayed cuff edge, exposing the same unusual teal-and-cream check.

clues.title: Ring beneath Glass
clues.detail: The fresh circular ring beneath the glass is crossed by a narrow blood droplet from the attack. Clara Haversham states that they set the glass down on the side table at 8:35 pm immediately before leaving the room.

clues.title: Crack in Plate
clues.detail: A crack runs across the plate. A continuous yellowed glue line is visible deep inside the break.

solution.motive: Victor Langford killed Eleanor Whitmore because the imminent publication of a damning review that would ruin his restaurant's reputation.
solution.method: Eleanor Whitmore was fatally stabbed with the knife.
solution.timeOfDeath: After 8:35 pm and before discovery at 8:45 pm
solution.opportunity: Victor Langford claimed to have left at 8:30 pm. The physical trace on the bottle corroborates the action described by Gareth Pembroke, while the trace on the glass corroborates the action described by Clara Haversham. They left together at 8:35 pm, and both traces are crossed by evidence from the later attack. Meanwhile, teal-and-cream tweed fibres on the knife directly contradict Victor Langford's denial and place them at the murder object during the attack window.
